event: Adoration of the Magi and Glorification of the Three Holy Kings
symbols: house-shaped shrine — the Church as the dwelling place of sacred relics and the heavenly Jerusalem
gold and precious stones — the glory and majesty of the Kingdom of God, echoing the heavenly city of Revelation 21
three kings depicted in relief — the Magi who recognized the newborn King, representing the Gentile nations coming to worship Christ
Christ enthroned in the upper gable — the exalted Lord to whom every knee shall bow
standing saints and prophets in arcade — the communion of saints who bear witness across the ages
description: A magnificent gilded reliquary shrine in the shape of a miniature church or house, photographed against a dark background. The structure has two registers. The lower register features a row of standing figures in high relief set within an arcade of round arches, each figure holding attributes such as staffs or books, rendered in gilded and enameled metalwork. The upper triangular gable shows a central enthroned figure flanked by standing saints or angels, with a smaller figure in a roundel at the apex.
theological_description: The Shrine of the Three Holy Kings at Cologne Cathedral is one of the most important reliquaries in Christendom, believed to house the remains of the Magi who traveled from the East to worship the infant Christ (Matthew 2:1-12). Theologically, the Magi represent the universality of Christ's kingship: even pagan astrologers from distant lands recognized what Herod and Jerusalem did not. Their gifts of gold, frankincense, and myrrh prophetically declare Christ's identity as King, God, and sacrificial victim. The shrine's architectural form as a miniature church expresses the patristic conviction that the Church is the true house of God where Christ dwells among His people. The prophets and saints depicted on its walls testify to the continuity of salvation history — from the Old Testament patriarchs who foretold the Messiah to the apostles and saints who proclaimed Him. Nicholas of Verdun's masterwork thus becomes a theological statement in precious metal: the entire story of redemption converges on the Christ Child whom the Magi adored.

scripture_refs.reason: The visit of the Magi to the infant Jesus, bringing gifts of gold, frankincense, and myrrh — the central event the shrine commemorates
The kings of Tarshish and the isles shall bring presents; the kings of Sheba and Seba shall offer gifts — messianic psalm prefiguring the Magi's homage
Nations shall come to your light, and kings to the brightness of your rising — Isaiah's prophecy of Gentile kings bringing gold and frankincense
The heavenly Jerusalem adorned with gold and precious stones — the shrine's splendor echoes the eschatological city of God
